content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA and an integer XXX. You are allowed to perform the following operation on the array:


Select two **distinct** indices iii and jjj and set both AiA_iAi​ and AjA_jAj​ as ((Ai⊕Aj)∣X)((A_i \oplus A_j) \mid X)((Ai​⊕Aj​)∣X) simultaneously. Here ⊕\oplus⊕ and ∣\mid∣ denote the bitwise XOR and bitwise OR operations respectively.
Find the **minimum** number of operations required to make **all** elements of the array **odd**. If it is not possible to do so, print −1-1−1 instead.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN, the size of the array and XXX.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers denoting the elements of the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the **minimum** number of operations required to make **all** elements of the array **odd**. If it is not possible to do so, print −1-1−1 instead.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 10^5.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and X. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of operations required to make all array elements odd, or -1 if it is impossible.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: all numbers are already odd, so 0 operations.
Test case 2: X is even and there is at least one odd element. There are two even numbers (2 and 4), each needs one operation, so answer is 2.
X is odd. There are 4 even numbers. Each operation can fix two evens, so ceil(4/2)=2 operations are enough.
X is even and the array contains no odd element. No operation can produce an odd number, thus impossible.

hidden.label: edge case – already all odd, X even

solution.approach:
Let cntEven be the number of even elements and cntOdd = N - cntEven.

*If X is odd* (its least‑significant bit is 1), the operation always produces an odd number because (a xor b) | X has LSB = 1. One operation can turn two even numbers into odd numbers, so the optimal number of operations is the smallest number of pairs that cover all evens: ceil(cntEven / 2).

*If X is even* (LSB = 0), the parity of the result equals the parity of a xor b, i.e. it is odd only when the two chosen elements have different parity. Therefore:
  - If there is at least one odd element initially, each even element can be paired with an odd element (the odd element stays odd after the operation). This needs exactly cntEven operations.
  - If all elements are even, no operation can create an odd number, so the task is impossible.
The algorithm simply counts evens and odds and applies the above formulas, yielding O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (total O(sum of N) ≤ 10^5).
